author: @kimkeam2094
text: Gorilla glue requires you to lightly spray water on the surfaces to be bonded. I didn’t know when I bought the first bottle and thought it was rubbish. After applying water, so much better.

author: @philipcowzer8962
text:
Using can or liquid PU. Use a wet cloth on one surface and pu foam/liquid to other. Speeds up grab time and drying.
Tip use 'Everbuild' brand polyurethane wood adhesive from 'Toolstation' comes in 5min & 30min

author: @johnland7318
text:
Just retired from working as a technician in a High School DT department, and had to source a glue for Kingspan.....The Model Shop, Leeds had tubes of UHU POR, (yellow/green package) a safe for foam gluing mix which doesn't dissolve the foam. It is used by aero modellers forming wings. This glue, sticks everything - wood metal hard plastic stone paper card - either by holding together the join with wet glue until it dries (just like yellow tube UHU) or as a contact adhesive spread on both surfaces and wait until dry - bring together firmly. The glue line is not brittle like without CA glue. 
I prefer it over epoxy or CA for bonding different materials 95% time.
PS we used woodwork grade PVA to bond the foam - far cheaper and kept the UHU POR for technician and A-level work.

author: @f.d.6667
text: The "spread / wipe / leave open / then join" idea behind the Gorilla Glue (and similar 1K PU glues) is to give the un-cured resin time to absorb moisture from the air - and NOT to soak in the base material. A "soak-in" wouldn't make much sense as insulation PU foams are of the closed-cell type so there will not be any capillary effect. That's why you spray water on many of the expanding (PU) glues... my guess would be that water should be accelerating Gorilla/Wuerth glues quite a bit. (I am no chemist but I grew up in a household where we had isocyanate & polyols in our cleaning cupboard as my parents worked in a lab that tested and developed PU formulations).

author: @docink6175
text: I used to bond pu foam together in order to carve out props for the haunted house we operated for 20 years. The spray foam heats up and kind of melts into the pieces that it is bonding together. For a strong skin I used wrapping tissue paper and acrylic latex house paint, four or five layers and it was incredibly strong, it is also very weather resistant because the acrylic paint bonds with the tissue and forms almost a plastic

author: @petergambier
text:
Thanks once again Professor Robert, yet another winner done with your unique and entertaining style. Every builder I know swears by the NoNails brand but it hasn't helped fix what I needed, perhaps I used too much (or too little) or it wasn't clamped for long enough, it works fine with wood though.

As a calcium carbonate plasterer and mason I have learned to repair and carve in different stone's and normally use a Jolly's stone glue (plus a small tube of hardener) for most of the jobs.
I then tried a Gorilla glue and found it did a reasonable job but the ultimate testing ground for any product has to be my 2001 Berlingo because wet weather, the sun, wind and speed will really test it's staying power, or not.
 
I live in the countryside and with our narrow Somerset country roads all side mirrors will take a bit of battering, especially from cars coming towards you that don't want their clean paintwork scratched, that's why I drive an old banger and don't drive in the middle of the road.

Because of this my side mirrors also tend to take a beating and those that have narrow cars will have fewer repair bills instead.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: 'No Nails' comes in several varieties, some is 'water-based', some 'ester-based', some in various 'solvents'. You can dilute Thixofix' and get a good bond too, but it takes time to do it right. Taping and filleting the joints has some very useful effects.

replies.author: @petergambier

author: @robertwinsper7409
text:
Having mucked about with this stuff in the past we found that the issue with glueing the foam together was the ability to sand the desired shape across a joint. Some adhesives were very hard compared to the foam and the joint line would stand proud of the surface. The best option we found was two pack expanding foam in that it set quickly compared to the single pack squirty foam and was a better hardness match to the Kingspan/Recticell insulation boards . Hence it was easier to consistently sand across a joint. It was also better than squirty foam in that it would react completely in a confined space without additional water. The squirty foam in a confined space frequently had un reacted "Toffee" inside it , even after long setting periods.
The downside to using the two pack foam was having to make lots of little mixes of the stuff. Not very convenient and a bit wasteful. 
I'll watch this one closely and see how you get on.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Silicone kit will do. The type used in bathrooms and such. Not that bad. You can use a hot wire to cut that stuff. A friction cable as used for bicycle will do. Peel one single wire of from the inner cable, make a U shape from some wood, attach the wire like in a figure or scroll saw, put one or 2 amps through and it will cut this stuff like butter. A stretched small spring could do as well. Hardened steel thin wire works best.

author: @judyofthewoods
text: How serendipitous! Only yesterday I was thinking, how do I best glue PU foam that will fill gaps but not push out the parts to be glued, as the PU foam spray does quite significantly, in my experience. Also, if I remember rightly, the foam needs to be used up in one go (I won't need that much) as the nozzle tends to block up. I wanted to make a super-insulated box for off-grid food cooling,  that has no thermal bridge to a) warm the content, and b) cause condensation on the cold parts to drip through to the outer wooden casing. Gorilla glue has my vote for a gapless construction.

author: @401ksolar
text: What I just watched was a demonstration of cohesion versus adhesion.(by using the same foam, the solvent which turns to gas allows for the two foam blocks to cohere, whereas adhesives have differing solvents therefore no cohesive action occurs only adhesion, however with some surface punctures on both faces could provide even better strength for the joints?)
